story.headline: Texas makes a statement in 47-21 rout of Washington
body: Texas dominated from start to finish in a 47-21 rout over Washington in Week 7 PCFL action, with Arch Manning earning Player of the Game honors after going for 20 of 30 for 285 yards and 4 TDs.
The Longhorns outgained Washington 517–331 in total offense, including 118 yards on the ground. Washington kept fighting, managing 331 total yards on the night.
Up next, Texas hosts Miami in Week 8.

aiStory.headline: Manning's Arm Overwhelms Washington in Dominant 47-21 Texas Victory
body: Arch Manning put on a clinic Saturday, threading passes with precision and anticipating coverage windows that left Washington's secondary scrambling. His four touchdown passes and 285 yards on efficient 20-of-30 accuracy established Texas's offensive identity early and never allowed the visiting Huskies a foothold. Texas built its lead methodically in the first half, converting short fields into scoring drives while forcing Washington into reactive football.

The turning point arrived in the second quarter when Manning hit Anderson for a touchdown on a perfectly placed third-and-seven throw down the sideline. That score pushed Texas to 24-7 and signaled to Washington that they were facing a quarterback executing at a level their defense could not match. From that point forward, the Huskies were playing catchup against an offense that had found its rhythm, and quarterback Williams Jr.'s efforts—two touchdowns of his own—became consolation rather than a genuine threat.

Texas's defense wasn't flashy but was sufficiently stout, with Biles recording a sack and anchoring the front. Washington managed 244 passing yards and 87 rushing yards, numbers that reflect an offense never quite able to establish dominance. The 26-point margin understates nothing: this was Texas asserting itself as a team playing a different speed than its opponent. Manning's control of the line of scrimmage and field position proved decisive.
aiStory.turningPoint: Anderson's sideline touchdown reception in the second quarter stretched Texas's lead to 24-7 and established Manning's efficiency as uncontainable.

unsungHero.name: Anderson
unsungHero.why: Seven receptions for 100 yards without a touchdown often gets overlooked, but Anderson's consistent separation and route-running gave Manning easy completions that sustained drives when Washington needed stops most.

story.headline: Arkansas pulls away from UCLA, 27-17
body: Arkansas asserted control when it mattered in a 27-17 win over UCLA in Week 7 PCFL action, with Mike Washington earning Player of the Game honors after going for 96 rushing yards and 2 rushing TDs.
The Razorbacks outgained UCLA 443–366 in total offense, including 141 yards on the ground. UCLA kept fighting, managing 366 total yards on the night.
Up next, Arkansas travels to face Clemson in Week 8.

aiStory.headline: Arkansas grinds past UCLA with Washington's two-touchdown day
body: Arkansas controlled the line of scrimmage from the opening drive, and that dominance told the story in a 27-17 victory over UCLA on Saturday. Mike Washington's two-touchdown rushing performance anchored a ground game that suffocated UCLA's offense when it mattered most. The Bruins came in with a passing attack ranked among the conference's best, and they had the numbers to prove it—343 yards through the air—but those yards came in bunches too disconnected to mount sustained pressure. Arkansas's defense bent but rarely broke, forcing UCLA into third-and-long situations where the Bruins' inability to generate running game success became their undoing.

The telling number sits in the rushing column: Arkansas 141 yards to UCLA's 23. That 118-yard gap represents the fundamental difference between a team that could establish rhythm and one that never found it. Jackson's line doesn't jump off the page—20 of 35 with a single touchdown—but he managed the game with precision, taking what the defense gave and letting Washington's legs do the heavy lifting. UCLA's Iamaleava threw for 374 yards and two scores, putting together an efficient afternoon by traditional measures, but efficiency without results is just noise. Smith's six receptions for 123 yards felt like empty calories when the team trailed by two scores heading into the fourth quarter.

The second half exposed UCLA's structural weakness. Without a running game to lean on and facing a defense that brought consistent pressure up the middle, the Bruins became predictable. Washington's two touchdowns weren't the product of explosive plays but rather the consequence of Arkansas moving the chains methodically enough that the goal line became inevitable. This wasn't a clinic in offensive creativity. It was a masterclass in doing what works and daring the other team to stop it.
aiStory.turningPoint: With UCLA trailing by three early in the fourth quarter, Washington punched in his second touchdown on a short dive, extending Arkansas's lead to ten and effectively ending the Bruins' comeback window.

unsungHero.name: Pleasant
unsungHero.why: Pleasant's ten tackles and steady presence at linebacker slowed Arkansas's running attack at points when it could have been worse, but his efforts were buried by the team's offensive dysfunction and inability to create pressure on the edges.

story.headline: Boston College pulls away from Tennessee, 34-21
body: Boston College asserted control when it mattered in a 34-21 win over Tennessee in Week 7 PCFL action, with Sam Leavitt earning Player of the Game honors after going for 38 of 57 for 346 yards and 3 TDs.
The Eagles outgained Tennessee 421–280 in total offense, including 81 yards on the ground. Tennessee kept fighting, managing 280 total yards on the night.
Up next, Boston College hosts Oregon in Week 8.

aiStory.headline: Boston College's Leavitt proves too sharp; Tennessee's ground game runs out of time
body: Sam Leavitt carved up Tennessee's secondary with surgical precision, completing 38 of 57 passes for 346 yards and three touchdowns to lead Boston College to a 34-21 victory on the road. The Eagles' passing attack found consistent rhythm throughout the afternoon, with Leavitt spreading the ball across multiple receivers and managing the game without a single turnover—a discipline that proved decisive against a Tennessee defense that couldn't generate consistent pressure.

Tennessee's rushing attack, led by Bishop's 89 yards and two touchdown runs, showed genuine teeth on the ground. The Volunteers moved the ball in chunks during stretches and kept pace early, but the passing game became a fatal constraint. MacIntyre completed just 11 of 23 attempts for 203 yards, forcing Tennessee to lean heavily on Bishop rather than opening up the play-action concepts that might have loosened Boston College's secondary. A critical fumble late in the second half handed Boston College the ball in favorable field position and momentum it never relinquished.

Boston College's victory was built on efficiency and balance. While the Eagles managed only 81 rushing yards, they didn't need more; Leavitt's poise allowed Harris to accumulate eight receptions and 81 yards, and the offense converted third downs at a rate that kept Tennessee's defense constantly fatigued and on the field. Tennessee's defense, led by Anderson's six tackles, competed hard but lacked the help needed when the offense couldn't sustain drives or protect the quarterback. By the final quarter, Boston College's cushion had grown too wide for Tennessee to close.
aiStory.turningPoint: Tennessee's fumble in the third quarter shifted field position decisively in Boston College's favor and coincided with the Volunteers' offense stalling out completely, unable to answer with any scoring response.

unsungHero.name: Jefferson
unsungHero.why: Jefferson's 120 receiving yards on just three receptions showcased explosive playmaking that Tennessee desperately needed, yet the offense couldn't build sustained drives around his talent because MacIntyre's accuracy issues and the reliance on the run game left him isolated.

story.headline: No contest: LSU buries Clemson 41-10
body: LSU dominated from start to finish in a 41-10 rout over Clemson in Week 7 PCFL action, with Garrett Nussmeier earning Player of the Game honors after going for 24 of 38 for 287 yards and 3 TDs.
The Tigers outgained Clemson 525–184 in total offense, including 238 yards on the ground. Clemson never got the offense going, managing 184 total yards on the night.
Up next, LSU hosts UCLA in Week 8.

aiStory.headline: LSU's Ground Game Overwhelms Clemson in Lopsided Week 7 Rout
body: LSU's offensive line dictated terms from the opening kickoff, establishing a ground-based attack that Clemson's defense simply could not contain. Ferguson rushed for 100 yards on 13 carries while Nussmeier orchestrated a balanced assault that racked up 525 total yards. Clemson managed just 184 yards of offense and never found rhythm, with Cortez held to 14 completions on 28 attempts despite a late touchdown pass to Monroe.

The decisive margin came in field position and efficiency. LSU converted short fields into touchdowns three times, with Nussmeier's poise in the pocket allowing him to stretch plays vertically when the ground game drew defenders. Chapman's eight receptions for 123 yards gave LSU's passing game enough teeth to prevent Clemson from stacking the box entirely. Meanwhile, Clemson's offense stalled repeatedly, managing only 48 rushing yards and surrendering a fumble that LSU never needed to fully exploit.

Clemson's defensive unit, led by McFarland's 11 tackles, worked tirelessly but lacked support from an offense that couldn't sustain drives or control clock. The coaching room faces a critical reset heading into the stretch run, having fallen behind in all three phases against a team that executed its game plan with precision and patience.
aiStory.turningPoint: LSU's defense forced a Clemson fumble early in the second half and converted the turnover into Ferguson's second rushing touchdown, extending the lead to 27-3 and effectively deciding the contest.

unsungHero.name: Chapman
unsungHero.why: While Nussmeier earned Player of the Game honors, Chapman's eight catches for 123 yards were the glue that kept LSU's offense efficient and prevented Clemson from loading the box against Ferguson.

story.headline: Instant classic: Penn State edges Georgia, 29-26
body: Penn State found just enough in a 29-26 thriller over Georgia in Week 7 PCFL action, with Mallik Myers earning Player of the Game honors after going for 22 of 36 for 248 yards and 2 TDs.
The Nittany Lions were outgained by Georgia 335–327 in total offense, including 98 yards on the ground. Georgia kept fighting, managing 335 total yards on the night.
Up next, Penn State hosts Notre Dame in Week 8.

aiStory.headline: Penn State edges Georgia in turnover battle, Myers steady in upset bid
body: Penn State's defense forced the issue when it mattered most, converting Georgia's two fumbles into the margin of victory in a 29-26 win that hinged on possession discipline rather than yardage. Georgia actually out-gained Penn State 335 to 327 and held a slight edge in passing yards through 41 completions, yet the Bulldogs' two giveaways proved fatal in a game decided by three points.

Mallik Myers orchestrated the winning effort with surgical precision despite completing just 22 of 36 attempts. His two touchdown passes and lone interception-free performance gave Penn State's coaching room exactly what they needed: a quarterback who protected the football when turnovers were the currency of the day. Georgia's Giles matched Myers' touchdown total and threw for 254 yards, but the two fumbles—one recovered by each team—shifted momentum at critical junctures.

The passing game dominated the script. Georgia's Nash hauled in six catches for 94 yards, stretching the Penn State secondary repeatedly, while Penn State's Hinnant worked underneath routes to move the chains. Neither team found consistent success on the ground, with Penn State managing just 98 rushing yards and Georgia 90, forcing both offenses into third-down situations where execution separated them. Penn State converted when it counted; Georgia did not.
aiStory.turningPoint: Georgia's second fumble in the fourth quarter, recovered by Penn State deep in Georgia territory, eliminated the Bulldogs' comeback window and forced them into desperation mode with limited time remaining.

unsungHero.name: Kelsay
unsungHero.why: Six tackles and rock-solid coverage accountability in the secondary, without the sack or splashy play that garners headlines, but his consistency limited Georgia's explosive passing opportunities and kept drives manageable.

story.headline: Ohio State survives Miami by 3 in thriller
body: Ohio State found just enough in a 37-34 thriller over Miami in Week 7 PCFL action, with Julian Sayin earning Player of the Game honors after going for 19 of 30 for 256 yards and 2 TDs.
The Buckeyes outgained Miami 435–424 in total offense, including 185 yards on the ground. Miami kept fighting, managing 424 total yards on the night.
Up next, Ohio State hosts Colorado in Week 8.

aiStory.headline: Sayin's Poise Carries Ohio State Past Miami in Defensive Slugfest
body: Julian Sayin did not need to be perfect to beat Miami. He needed to be efficient, and that's exactly what the Ohio State quarterback delivered in a 37-34 victory that came down to field position and execution in tight moments. Sayin completed 19 of 30 passes for 256 yards and two touchdowns, leaning heavily on Ohio State's ground game and a defense that bent but didn't break when it mattered most. The Hurricanes pressed through the air with 318 passing yards and kept pace for nearly four quarters, yet the Buckeyes' ability to control the line of scrimmage—185 rushing yards to Miami's 106—proved the difference.

Miami's passing attack, orchestrated by Mertz's 31 completions on 51 attempts, kept the Hurricanes competitive and gave them chances to pull ahead late. The real damage came from Lilly in the backfield, who punched in two rushing touchdowns to keep Miami's hopes alive. Campen's six receptions for 84 yards showed Miami could move the ball underneath. But when the Hurricanes needed a yard or a score in the final moments, Ohio State's defense—anchored by Pierce's five tackles—held firm enough to preserve the win.

The Buckeyes' patience on offense was deliberate and methodical. Roberts broke off one carry for 47 yards that set up critical field position, while Henry's four receptions moved chains when Ohio State needed rhythm. The one costly fumble nearly derailed the effort, but Sayin's composure in the pocket and willingness to take what Miami's secondary gave him allowed Ohio State to convert just enough third downs. In a game where both teams wanted to run the ball and both defenses showed up to work, Ohio State simply made one or two more plays when it counted.
aiStory.turningPoint: Ohio State's conversion on third-and-7 with under four minutes remaining, capped by a Sayin touchdown pass, gave the Buckeyes a three-point cushion that Miami could not overcome despite Mertz's final drive.

unsungHero.name: Roberts
unsungHero.why: One carry for 47 yards doesn't fill the stat sheet, but that single run set field position and rhythm in a game Ohio State won by three—Roberts' explosiveness forced Miami's defense to respect the threat and opened passing lanes for Sayin.

story.headline: Notre Dame takes control late, tops Michigan 35-19
body: Notre Dame asserted control when it mattered in a 35-19 win over Michigan in Week 7 PCFL action, with CJ Carr earning Player of the Game honors after going for 26 of 42 for 349 yards and 3 TDs.
The Fighting Irish outgained Michigan 504–233 in total offense, including 163 yards on the ground. Michigan kept fighting, managing 233 total yards on the night.
Up next, Notre Dame travels to face Penn State in Week 8.

aiStory.headline: Notre Dame's ground game breaks through in dominant Week 7 statement win
body: Notre Dame's offensive line imposed its will early and often, establishing a ground presence that Michigan's defense simply could not contain. While the final rushing yardage totals don't scream dominance at 163 yards, the efficiency and timing of those carries set up play-action opportunities that left Michigan's secondary in constant flux. CJ Carr completed 26 of 42 passes for 349 yards and three touchdowns, but the true story was how Notre Dame's staff engineered an offense that kept Michigan off balance. The Irish moved the ball with a methodical confidence that suggested they understood exactly what the Wolverines were trying to do.

Michigan's passing attack, led by QB Browne's 255 yards and two scores, showed flashes of competence, particularly through the arm of WR Wendtland, who hauled in 76 yards on just two receptions. Yet the turnovers proved catastrophic. Two fumbles lost when Notre Dame surrendered none told the entire subplot. Michigan's run game evaporated for just three yards—a number that speaks to how thoroughly the Irish front seven controlled the line of scrimmage. Brock managed only four carries, a clear indicator that Michigan's offensive line never found traction against a Notre Dame defense that smelled opportunity and capitalized.

The 16-point margin obscures how thoroughly Notre Dame controlled field position and tempo. This wasn't a shootout or a back-and-forth affair. It was methodical, disciplined football from the staff that refused to beat itself while systematically beating Michigan. In a season where margins matter, Notre Dame demonstrated the kind of complementary football that separates contenders from pretenders.
aiStory.turningPoint: Notre Dame's defense forced a fumble late in the second quarter with the game still within reach for Michigan, and the Irish scored immediately after to establish a two-touchdown lead they never relinquished.

unsungHero.name: DEF Manning
unsungHero.why: Four tackles and a sack don't jump off the stat sheet in a 35-19 game, but Manning's disruptive presence in Michigan's backfield set the tone for Notre Dame's defensive consistency when it mattered most.

story.headline: Oklahoma pulls away from Colorado, 22-10
body: Oklahoma asserted control when it mattered in a 22-10 win over Colorado in Week 7 PCFL action, with Keith Rocha earning Player of the Game honors.
The Sooners outgained Colorado 383–359 in total offense, including 127 yards on the ground. Colorado kept fighting, managing 359 total yards on the night.
Up next, Oklahoma hosts Tennessee in Week 8.

aiStory.headline: Oklahoma's defense strangles Colorado in low-scoring battle
body: Oklahoma's 22-10 victory over Colorado on Saturday was a masterclass in defensive suffocation, even if the scoreboard never quite reflected the completeness of the performance. The Sooners held Colorado to just 10 points despite the Buffaloes outgaining them in passing yards 306 to 256, a sign that the visiting defense made crucial stops when yardage threatened to become points. Colorado's two fumbles—both of which Oklahoma recovered—ultimately proved the difference in a game that turned into a field position battle.

QB Glass managed the game without spectacular numbers, completing 28 of 42 passes for 266 yards as Oklahoma's offense leaned on a steadier diet of runs. That conservative approach yielded just 127 rushing yards, yet it controlled clock and tempo in a way that limited Colorado's possessions. The Buffaloes countered with Anderson's 300-yard effort, including a touchdown throw, and Golden's reliable seven receptions for 94 yards suggested they had weapons Oklahoma's secondary couldn't completely shut down. Yet those yard totals proved deceiving—Colorado never found the end zone again after one score.

The game's real narrative belonged to the kicking game and turnover margin. Keith Rocha's four field goals accounted for nearly all of Oklahoma's production, and while that doesn't sound like championship football, it underscores how well the Sooners' defense corralled Anderson and the Buffaloes' receivers in the red zone. Colorado's inability to protect the football—two lost fumbles against zero—essentially handed Oklahoma the game in the fourth quarter when field position mattered most.
aiStory.turningPoint: Colorado's second fumble midway through the fourth quarter, recovered by Oklahoma's defense deep in Buffaloes territory, ended any momentum the visitors had built and forced them into a two-possession deficit with time running short.

unsungHero.name: Phillips
unsungHero.why: His six tackles anchored Colorado's defense and kept the Buffaloes competitive despite the offense struggling to convert yardage into points, yet the turnovers and field goal drought overshadowed his steady work on the backend.

story.headline: No contest: Oregon buries USC 47-26
body: Oregon dominated from start to finish in a 47-26 rout over USC in Week 7 PCFL action, with Brock Thomas earning Player of the Game honors after going for 25 of 37 for 425 yards and 2 TDs.
The Ducks outgained USC 556–258 in total offense, including 135 yards on the ground. USC kept fighting, managing 258 total yards on the night.
Up next, Oregon travels to face Boston College in Week 8.

aiStory.headline: Oregon's passing attack overwhelms USC in lopsided Pac clash
body: Brock Thomas had the kind of afternoon that makes defensive coordinators lose sleep. The Oregon quarterback threaded passes with precision across a vulnerable USC secondary, completing 25 of 37 attempts for 425 yards and two touchdowns in a 47-26 rout that was never truly competitive after the opening quarter. Thomas's composure in the pocket and willingness to let plays develop downfield exposed gaps in USC's coverage scheme that the Trojan defense simply could not adjust to over four quarters.

The disparity in total offense told the real story: Oregon accumulated 556 yards to USC's meager 258, a gap that reflected fundamental differences in execution and game plan. While Oregon's ground game remained pedestrian with just 135 rushing yards, the passing attack more than compensated. Wide receiver Perry's five catches for 121 yards and consistent intermediate routes kept chains moving and kept USC's defense on its heels. USC quarterback Moss threw for 226 yards and three touchdowns, respectable numbers on the surface, but he spent much of the day operating in catch-up mode, forced into a rhythm that favored quick passes over systematic drives.

Oregon's turnover discipline proved crucial where it mattered most. Zero turnovers allowed the Ducks to control tempo and field position without self-inflicted wounds. USC's one fumble, meanwhile, punctuated an afternoon of missed opportunities and mounting frustration. The coaching staff's ability to keep Thomas upright and running a balanced attack—even when it didn't always show in rushing statistics—gave Oregon the structural advantage that turned a conference matchup into a statement victory.
aiStory.turningPoint: In the second quarter, Thomas connected with Perry on a 34-yard gain that set up a touchdown and effectively put USC into an unrecoverable deficit from which their defense never recovered.

unsungHero.name: Perry
unsungHero.why: Thomas gets the spotlight for his quarterback excellence, but Perry's five receptions for 121 yards provided the reliable target option that allowed Oregon to attack vertically and control the game's tempo without needing the ground game.

weeklyWrap.headline: Texas dominates the arc; LSU and Penn State shake the table
weeklyWrap.subhead: Week 7 clarified the hierarchy at the top while upending assumptions about contenders in the middle.
weeklyWrap.lead: Texas rolled through Washington with the kind of surgical efficiency that separates the pretenders from the genuine threats, reinforcing its perch atop the power poll. But the real story of the week belonged to the teams willing to upset the established order: Penn State stole a thriller from Georgia on the road, and LSU dismantled Clemson in a statement that cannot be ignored.

segments.title: Texas methodical, Texas relentless, Texas elite
segments.body: A 26-point margin does not capture the suffocation. Texas controlled the line of scrimmage from kickoff, dictating tempo and turning what could have been a competitive Pac matchup into a coronation. This is the kind of win that makes voters and analysts nod knowingly: not flashy, not desperate, just complete.

segments.title: Penn State steals a road classic from Georgia
segments.body: Georgia came in ranked third by the power poll and left with a three-point loss in a game that will replay in highlight reels for weeks. Penn State's composure down the stretch, especially in a one-score environment, suggested a team that has learned how to win close. This is the kind of road victory that either launches a season or becomes a bitter what-if.

segments.title: LSU dismantles Clemson in rout of the week
segments.body: Clemson's defense came in expecting to impose its will and instead watched LSU score 41 points with the kind of ease that suggests a talent gap. The 31-point margin tells you everything: this was not a close game that got away, but a total domination that vaults LSU into genuine contender conversation regardless of where the power poll had it.

segments.title: Ohio State and Miami trade haymakers in shootout
segments.body: A three-point game that saw 71 total points belongs in the conversation for best offensive performances of the week. Both teams scored in the 30s, both moved the ball at will, and Ohio State's ability to land the final punch in a wide-open duel suggests poise when the stakes elevate.

segments.title: Oregon's rout of USC resets the West
segments.body: A 21-point victory erases any doubt about who holds the upper hand in that pairing. Oregon's dominant performance—47 points on display—signals that the Pac has a clear leader, and USC will have work to do if it wants to remain in the playoff conversation as the season tightens.

weeklyWrap.closer: Next week's slate will test whether these rising teams can sustain momentum or whether Week 7's surprises prove fleeting.
